metadata.title: Verification Matrix: Match the Check to the Output
metadata.description_blurb: Proofreading is not auditing — each output type requires a different evidence check, and the verification matrix tells you which one before you sign off.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: She asked Claude to summarize five remote-work productivity papers — with citations. The summary was fluent, well-structured, and confident. She read it for flow. Two of the five citations pointed to papers that do not exist. The authors are real. The titles are plausible. The papers are not. She had proofread the output. She had not audited it. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, summarize five papers with citations

props.command: Summarize these five remote-work productivity papers with citations.

beats.narration_text: Fluency is a training artifact, not an accuracy signal. Claude is trained to produce confident, human-like text. A hallucinated citation matches the fluency of a real one — same structure, same scholarly register, same year format. Semantic entropy research shows you cannot distinguish fabricated from verified content from surface features alone. Editing for clarity does not catch this. Auditing for evidence does.
beats.new_visual_element: Fluency ≠ accuracy — hallucinated citation matches surface of real one / must audit not just edit

beats.narration_text: Editing asks: is this clear, coherent, well-organized? Auditing asks: is this true, traceable, and supported by the source? Most people only edit. The verification matrix assigns the right check to each output type before you review. For citations: open the source, confirm title and author, verify it says what Claude claims. For numbers and tables: recompute a sample, check the denominator logic. Format and fluency do not enter.
beats.new_visual_element: Edit vs. audit — clear/coherent vs. true/traceable / matrix assigns check by output type
beats.spark_line: Edit for clarity. Audit for truth.

beats.narration_text: The gate depth matches the consequence. Three tiers: light — personal notes, draft outlines, brainstorms — scan for obvious errors, do not recompute. Moderate — internal presentations, planning documents — spot-check claims and figures. Strict — published work, submitted documents, anything you sign — full audit before use. A client-facing email is strict, not low, because the downstream consequence is yours.
beats.new_visual_element: Risk-tiered gate — light/moderate/strict / gate depth matches consequence / client email = strict
beats.spark_line: Match the gate to the consequence.

beats.narration_text: The rule is one sentence: no verification path, no delegation. If you cannot describe how you would check the output before you delegate the task, you are outsourcing your judgment, not your labor. The verification matrix makes this concrete — it lists, by output type, the evidence you need before the output leaves your hands. That list does not change because the prose sounds confident.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — verification matrix: output type / check method / evidence required / gate tier

beats.narration_text: Before your next delegation, build your verification path. Paste this.

props.command: Before you generate anything: I need to verify this output. For each component, tell me (1) what type of claim it makes — factual, numerical, citation, or synthesis — (2) what evidence I would need to confirm it is correct, and (3) what I should check first if I have limited time. Then generate the output. [describe the task]

beats.narration_text: If this keeps a hallucinated citation from reaching a document it should not be in, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
